source.title: Multiple factors based adaptive laboratory evolution strategy enhances DHA production in Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 by rewiring key metabolic pathways

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: Wild-type Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 has low DHA yield, partly due to poor adaptation to low pH during fermentation. The study aims to enhance DHA production via adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) using multiple stress factors (low pH, low temperature, high DO) to rewire metabolism.

section_gists.heading: Abstract
section_gists.gist: Staged acidic ALE with optimized oxygen and temperature increased DHA concentration by 171.4%. Transcriptomics revealed upregulation of glycolysis, PKS pathway, TCA cycle, and PPP, with differential ATP/NADPH supply. GK upregulation suggests glycerol utilization potential.
process_signals: Wild-type strains fail commercial DHA yields due to poor low-pH adaptation.
ALE strategy combined low pH, low temperature, high DO.
DHA increased 171.4%, biomass 106.3%, TFA 243.8%.
Transcriptomic rewiring of central carbon and lipid metabolism observed.

section_gists.heading: Background
section_gists.gist: Thraustochytrids are promising DHA producers but wild-type yields are insufficient. ALE with single stressors has improved traits; synergistic multi-stress ALE (low pH, low temperature, high DO) is unexplored. Hypothesis: multi-factor acclimation involves complex metabolic rewiring.
process_signals: DHA from fish oil has drawbacks; thraustochytrids are alternative.
Wild-type DHA yields fall short commercially.
ALE with single stressors (temperature, DO, NaCl) improved biomass and lipids.
Synergistic effects of >2 stressors unexplored.
Fermentation pH drops due to organic acids, impairing growth.
Low-temperature ALE upregulates cold-resistance genes; high-DO ALE triggers antioxidant defenses.
Hypothesis: multi-factor acclimation rewires metabolism for higher DHA.
failures_or_limits: Wild-type strains poorly adapted to low pH.

section_gists.heading: Adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE)
section_gists.gist: Orthogonal design combined two temperatures (16°C, 28°C), two DO levels (170 rpm, 230 rpm), and three acid types (citric, acetic, HCl) for 12 ALE conditions. Staged acid ALE: pH decreased from 5.5 to 3.5 over 75 passages (150 days).
process_signals: Preliminary data: low temperature (16°C) and high DO (230 rpm) increase DHA.
Orthogonal experimental design to optimize workload.
Staged method: 15 passages per pH stage, total 75 passages over 150 days.
Glycerol preservation monthly.

section_gists.heading: Triple-stress adaptive evolution synergistically enhances biomass and lipid productivity
section_gists.gist: Citric acid acclimation gave higher biomass than acetic or HCl. Triple stress (citric acid, 16°C, 230 rpm) strain ALE_AC_11 had highest biomass (12.4 g/L, +106.7%) and TFA (5.5 g/L, +243.8%). Low temperature/high DO alone did not further increase biomass over citric acid alone, but increased TFA.
process_signals: Citric acid strains had higher biomass; organic acid tolerance/remobilization may play a role.
Preliminary work: neither wild-type nor evolved strain grew on citric acid as sole carbon source.
Adding low temperature or high DO alone to citric acid did not further increase biomass, contrasting with previous high-DO ALE study.
Triple stress (ALE_AC_11) gave highest biomass and TFA.
Low temperature/high DO increased TFA but not biomass, confirming they enhance fatty acid production via cellular physiology.
methods: Flask fermentation under normal conditions (28°C, 170 rpm, M4 medium) for 72 h
failures_or_limits: Citric acid utilization as carbon source was limited (Fig. S2).
Low temperature or high DO alone did not enhance biomass beyond citric acid alone.

section_gists.heading: Acid-adaptive acclimation shifts lipid biosynthesis toward total fatty acid accumulation
section_gists.gist: DHA/TFA fraction decreased in most ALE strains except two (citric acid and acetic acid at normal conditions). Acid acclimation with low temperature/high DO improved TFA more than DHA, suggesting these conditions favor TFA over DHA production. Future work needed to enhance DHA proportion.
process_signals: DHA/TFA decreased in most ALE strains; only citric and acetic acid at normal conditions increased DHA fraction.
High DO may cause oxidative degradation of DHA.
Synergistic acclimation conditions more effective for TFA than DHA.
Future studies should focus on enhancing DHA proportion and analyzing SFA/PUFA ratios.
failures_or_limits: Decreased DHA/TFA in most ALE strains.
High DO may reduce DHA content due to peroxidation.
Need to investigate SFA/PUFA ratio and compositional lipid profiles.

section_gists.heading: Comparative transcriptomic profiling between the ALE strain and wild-type strain
section_gists.gist: Transcriptomes of ALE_AC_11 and wild-type compared at 36 h and 48 h. 23,118 DEGs at 36 h (14,568 up), 18,372 at 48 h (11,462 up). DEGs mapped to KEGG; focused on carbohydrate and lipid metabolism.
process_signals: Samples collected at 36 h (metabolic peak) and 48 h (decline).
Genome not sequenced; annotation based on homology.
eggNOG annotated most unigenes (58.33%).
DEGs decreased from 36 h to 48 h.
KEGG pathway enrichment analysis performed.
failures_or_limits: Genome not sequenced; reliance on homology-based annotation.

section_gists.heading: Early-stage metabolic reprogramming in the ALE strain: enhanced carbon metabolism and fatty acid biosynthesis priming
section_gists.gist: At 36 h, ALE_AC_11 showed upregulation of glycolysis, TCA cycle, oxidative phosphorylation, PPP (G6PD, 6PGD), and PKS pathway. Downregulation of terpenoid synthesis and AGPAT. GK and GPD upregulated despite no glycerol in medium, suggesting metabolic readiness.
process_signals: Pathway enrichment: carbohydrate, amino acid, lipid, energy metabolism, translation, folding/sorting/degradation.
Glycolysis, ME, PDH upregulated; PC downregulated → enhanced acetyl-CoA.
TCA cycle and ATP synthase upregulated → increased ATP.
PPP upregulated → increased NADPH.
GK and GPD upregulated → potential glycerol utilization or internal synthesis.
AGPAT downregulated → may inhibit TAG synthesis, redirect flux to fatty acids.
Terpenoid synthesis downregulated → reduced competition for acetyl-CoA.
PKS pathway upregulated.
failures_or_limits: Glycerol not in medium; origin of glycerol metabolism upregulation unclear.

section_gists.heading: Late-stage metabolic adaptation in the optimal ALE strain: balancing energy supply and fatty acid accumulation
section_gists.gist: At 48 h, lower glycolysis remained efficient, but PFK downregulated and G6PD upregulated, shifting flux to PPP and decreasing ATP/NADPH ratio. PKS still upregulated. β-oxidation genes upregulated, possibly to compensate ATP. GK and GPD still upregulated.
process_signals: PFK downregulation at 48 h (not at 36 h) → decreased upper glycolysis flux.
G6PD upregulation → increased PPP flux.
ATP synthase genes downregulated → lower ATP supply.
PKS A, B, C still upregulated.
β-oxidation genes (ACAD, ACOX) upregulated, but TFA still higher in ALE strain.
Hypothesis: β-oxidation provides ATP to rebalance with NADPH.
GK and GPD consistently upregulated; potential for glycerol utilization.
failures_or_limits: Reason for increased β-oxidation unclear; hypothesized ATP compensation.

section_gists.heading: Metabolic transition from early to late stages in the optimal ALE strain
section_gists.gist: ALE_AC_11 had 4797 DEGs from 36 h to 48 h (mostly downregulated), while wild-type had only 9. RNAP downregulated in ALE strain, indicating earlier transcriptional decline. ALE strain grew faster initially, entered stationary phase earlier, but still had favorable lipid traits at 48 h.
process_signals: ALE strain showed massive transcriptional downregulation from 36 to 48 h; wild-type did not.
RNAP downregulation suggests decline in transcriptional activity.
ALE strain growth advantage most pronounced in early-mid fermentation.
Biomass eventually similar due to resource limitation.
Genetic mutations (e.g., RNAP subunits) hypothesized but not identified.
failures_or_limits: Specific genetic mutations not determined; whole-genome sequencing needed.
Metabolic advantage diminishes in late fermentation.

section_gists.heading: Bioreactor validation of improved fermentation performance
section_gists.gist: 5-L bioreactor with multi-stage process: ALE_AC_11 had 17.9% higher biomass (71.1 vs 60.3 g/L), 25.8% higher TFA (28.3 vs 22.5 g/L), 22.1% higher DHA (9.4 vs 7.7 g/L) at 120 h. Differences smaller than in flasks, possibly due to scale effects (oxygen transfer, mixing).
process_signals: Bioreactor achieved high-density fermentation.
ALE strain grew faster initially, entered stationary phase after 96 h.
TFA and DHA trends consistent with biomass.
DHA increase disproportionate to biomass, indicating improved biosynthesis efficiency.
Scale-up reduced productivity gain compared to flasks.
Future optimization of bioreactor conditions needed.
failures_or_limits: Smaller differences in bioreactor than flasks; potential oxygen transfer and mixing limitations.

section_gists.heading: Conclusions
section_gists.gist: Novel multi-stress ALE strategy (high DO, low temperature, citric acid) enhanced biomass, TFA, DHA. Transcriptomics revealed upregulation of PKS, ATP/NADPH/acetyl-CoA supply, suppression of competing pathways, and GK activation. Glycerol supplementation further increased lipid yield. Foundation for future genetic engineering.
process_signals: Acclimated strain showed substantial yield improvements.
Metabolic reprogramming confirmed by transcriptomics.
Glycerol utilization potential demonstrated.
Strategy provides basis for future metabolic engineering.
failures_or_limits: Future work needed on genetic mutations and bioreactor optimization.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: Wild-type Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 has low DHA yield due to poor acid tolerance.
Combined stress adaptation (low pH, low temperature, high DO) can synergistically rewire metabolism to enhance DHA production.
ALE with citric acid is more effective than acetic or hydrochloric acid due to organic acid tolerance/remobilization.
Transcriptomic reprogramming involves upregulation of glycolysis, TCA cycle, PPP, and PKS pathway, with temporal shifts in ATP/NADPH supply.
β-oxidation may compensate ATP in late fermentation.
GK upregulation indicates potential for glycerol utilization.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Specific genetic mutations responsible for transcriptional changes.
Origin of glycerol metabolism upregulation in absence of glycerol.
Mechanism of increased β-oxidation in late stage.
Why DHA/TFA fraction decreased in most ALE strains.
Scale-up challenges: oxygen transfer and mixing limitations in bioreactor.
Potential for further DHA proportion enhancement.
Role of SFA/PUFA ratio and compositional lipid profiles.

events.summary: Wild-type Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 fails to meet commercial DHA yields due to poor adaptation to low pH during fermentation.
Hypothesis that multi-factor acclimation (low pH, low temperature, high DO) will synergistically enhance DHA production through complex metabolic rewiring.
Orthogonal experimental design used to combine two temperatures, two DO levels, and three acid types for 12 ALE conditions.
Staged acidic ALE process: pH decreased from 5.5 to 3.5 over 75 passages (150 days).
No suitable reference genome available for Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16, necessitating de novo transcriptome assembly.

events.summary: Wild-type and 12 ALE endpoint strains cultured under standard conditions to measure DCW, TFA, DHA.
methods_or_tools: standard fermentation conditions (28 °C, 170 rpm, M4 medium)

events.summary: Citric acid acclimated strains showed higher biomass than other acids, indicating organic acid tolerance role.
Low temperature or high DO alone did not further increase biomass beyond citric acid alone.
Low temperature and high DO increased TFA but not biomass, confirming they enhance fatty acid production via cellular physiology.
DHA/TFA fraction decreased in most ALE strains; only citric and acetic acid at normal conditions increased DHA fraction.
High DO may cause oxidative degradation of DHA, reducing DHA/TFA fraction.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains key experimental results on biomass, TFA, DHA yields under various ALE conditions, and analysis of acid type effects and DHA/TFA fraction changes.

events.summary: Transcriptomic analysis of ALE_AC_11 and wild-type strains at 36 h and 48 h to identify differentially expressed genes.
KEGG pathway enrichment analysis of DEGs at 36 h revealed enrichment in carbohydrate, amino acid, lipid, and energy metabolism.
Genome not sequenced; annotation relied on homology-based methods, limiting identification of genetic mutations.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers transcriptomic analysis and early/late metabolic reprogramming in ALE strain, directly supporting the paper's core findings.

events.summary: KEGG pathway enrichment analysis of ALE_AC_11 vs wild-type at 36 h shows enrichment in carbohydrate, amino acid, lipid, and energy metabolism.
Upregulation of GK and GPD in ALE_AC_11 despite no glycerol in medium suggests potential internal glycerol synthesis or metabolic readiness.
Downregulation of AGPAT and terpenoid synthesis genes in ALE_AC_11 suggests redirection of acetyl-CoA flux toward fatty acid synthesis.
At 48 h, PFK downregulation and G6PD upregulation in ALE_AC_11 suggest decreased upper glycolysis flux and increased PPP flux, lowering ATP/NADPH ratio.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains detailed transcriptomic analysis of early and late fermentation stages, supporting metabolic rewiring hypotheses.

events.summary: Specific genetic mutations responsible for transcriptional changes remain undetermined; whole-genome sequencing needed.
Disproportionate DHA increase suggests ALE improved DHA biosynthesis efficiency per unit biomass, not just biomass accumulation.
Bioreactor productivity gains were smaller than flask cultures, possibly due to reduced oxygen transfer and mixing limitations.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers metabolic transition analysis, bioreactor validation, and conclusions. Events focus on transcriptomic decline, RNAP hypothesis, bioreactor performance, and scale-up limitations.
This chunk contains only reference list entries and publisher's note; no research process events are described.

evidence_bank.quote: wild-type strains isolated from natural environments typically fail to meet commercial demands for DHA yields, partly because they are poorly adapted to the decreased pH conditions encountered during fermentation.
we hypothesize multi-factor acclimation for DHA improvements would involve complex metabolic rewiring that awaits dissection.

we used orthogonal experimental design to combine two levels of temperature (16 °C and 28 °C), two levels of DO indicated by the shaking speed (170 rpm and 230 rpm), and three acid types (citric acid, acetic acid, and hydrochloric acid) to obtain 12 combinations of ALE conditions
The entire ALE process involves 75 passages over a period of 150 days.
Since a suitable reference genome is not available for the Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 strain used in this study, a reference-free (de novo) transcriptome assembly strategy was employed.

the wild-type strain and 12 ALE endpoint strains (Additional file 1: Table S1) were cultured under standard fermentation conditions (28 °C, 170 rpm, M4 medium) in triplicate. After 72 h of cultivation, these three indicators were measured.
Strains acclimated with citric acid generally showed higher biomass than those acclimated with other acids
This indicates that adaptability to specific acid types, rather than just a low pH value, contributes to improving biomass yield, suggesting a role of organic acid tolerance and/or remobilization
Compared with using citric acid alone as the acclimation condition, the addition of low-temperature or high-DO stress alone cannot further enhance the biomass accumulation ability.

applying low-temperature or high-DO stress alone significantly increased the TFA concentration in the ALE strain (Fig. 1A). This outcome starkly contrasts with the previous biomass results, further confirming that low-temperature and high-DO conditions enhance fatty acid production by maintaining normal cellular physiology and energy metabolism

the DHA/TFA fraction in most ALE strains decreased compared to that in the wild-type strain (42.3%), and increased in only two strains that were obtained through the citric acid (47.0%) and acetic acid (51.6%) acclimation at the normal temperature and oxygen level
Given that high DO is associated with oxidative stress, and DHA is peroxidation-sensitive, the decreased DHA/TFA fraction in high-DO strains could be partly attributed to oxidative degradation.
After 36 h of cultivation, a total of 23,118 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were identified
1,113 genes were enriched in carbohydrate metabolism, 979 genes were enriched in amino acid metabolism, 707 genes were enriched in lipid metabolism, and 618 genes were enriched in energy metabolism
While the Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 genome has not been sequenced, genes of other strains provide annotation references based on sequence homology.
1,113 genes were enriched in carbohydrate metabolism, 979 genes were enriched in amino acid metabolism, 707 genes were enriched in lipid metabolism, and 618 genes were enriched in energy metabolism

the up-regulated expression of glycerol kinase (GK) and glycerol-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GPD) coding genes suggest a potential of increase in the amount of glycerol-3-phosphate that enters the glycolytic pathway
down-regulation of the 1-acyl-glycerol-3-phosphate-O-acyltransferase (AGPAT) gene may indicate that acclimation can inhibit the triacylglycerol (TAG) synthesis pathway

the down-regulation of 6-phosphofructokinase (PFK), which is not observed at 36 h, along with the up-regulation of G6PD, indicate decreased flux through the upper glycolysis while increased flux through the pentose phosphate pathway
implying a decreased ratio of ATP/NADPH supplies
we acknowledge that the specific genetic mutations responsible for these transcriptional changes remain to be determined
the disproportionate rise in DHA yield suggests that ALE also improved DHA biosynthesis efficiency per unit biomass

the differences in biomass and DHA productivity with the 5-L bioreactor cultures are much smaller than those observed with small-scale flask cultures. This can be attributed to a lower productivity gain in the ALE strain, potentially due to reduced oxygen transfer, limited resource mixing, and other scale effects in the bioreactor
Use of adaptive laboratory evolution to discover key mutations enabling rapid growth of Escherichia coli K-12 MG1655 on glucose minimal medium.

M4\_36 and M4\_48 represent wild-type cells incubated for 36 h and 48 h using M4 medium, respectively; M4C\_36 and M4C\_48 denote the acclimated strain ALE\_AC\_11 sampled after 36 h and 48 h of incubation

Importantly, the ALE\_AC\_11 strain exhibited significantly lower expression of genes encoding RNA polymerase (RNAP) at 48 h than 36 h, indicating a potential decline in transcriptional activity during this metabolic transition period.

In previous single-factor and dual-factor acclimation experiments $[70]$ , the yield of DHA, though moderately enhanced compared to wild-type strains, exhibited no statistically significant improvement.
We hypothesize that similar mutations could also occur in the ALE\_AC\_11 strain, contributing to its enhanced lipid production and stress tolerance phenotypes.

However, there are also studies indicating that acidic acclimation conditions can lead to mutations in a subunit of RNAP (encoded by the rpoC gene) [64], and this subunit is also considered a global regulator of gene expression.

\ coli$ strains after high-temperature acclimation mainly improve their tolerance to high and low temperatures through mutations in two adaptive pathways, namely mutations in the $\beta$ subunit gene (rpoB) of RNAP and mutations in the termination factor rho of RNAP [63].

Previous research shows that $[62]$ through mutant screening and ALE methods of culturing with high-concentration carbon dioxide, the photosynthesis, carbon fixation, and glycolysis pathways of Haematococcus pluvialis can be enhanced.

However, one question remains open: what exactly is the reason for the increase in $\beta$ -oxidation levels in the late fermentation stage? The activity of the $\beta$ -oxidation pathway has been proposed to provide higher levels of ATP for fatty acid synthesis [54].

These results indicate that the main mechanism for increasing the yield of TFA and DHA in the optimal ALE strain at 48 h of fermentation was the increase of acetyl-CoA, NADPH and PKS synthesis pathway fluxes.
In previous studies, acclimated strains under acidic conditions usually showed a gradual loss of lysine decarboxylase (a major acid stress enzyme) activity [61].
In addition, the activity of the mitochondrial electron transport chain (ETC) will be enhanced under high-DO conditions $[60]$ , resulting in an increase in the supply of acetyl-CoA and ATP.

Most enzymes in glycolysis for pyruvate generation along with the malic enzyme (ME) that catalyzes the conversion of malate to pyruvate and the pyruvate dehydrogenase (PDH) that catalyzes the conversion of pyruvate to acetyl-CoA were up-regulated while pyruvate carboxylase (PC) that catalyzes the conversion of pyruvate to oxalacetate was down-regulated in the ALE\_AC\_11 strain compared to the wild-type strain, suggesting enhanced production of acetyl-CoA as the precursor of fatty acid synthesis.

These data suggest an activity in the carbohydrate metabolism, amino acid metabolism, energy metabolism, and lipid metabolism in the ALE\_AC\_11 strain than the wild-type strain during the early fermentation stage.

To further investigate the functions of these DEGs, we mapped them to relevant metabolic pathways in the KEGG database and screened out DEGs with q < 0.05 as significantly differentially expressed genes for further analysis.

For high-quality RNA samples, $2\mu \mathrm{g}$ of total RNA was used to construct an RNA library, and deep sequencing analysis was performed on the Illumina HiSeq 2500 sequencing platform.

Neutralizing with alkalis is costly, making ALE targeting low pH a practical alternative, as it enhances acid tolerance, stabilizing enzyme activity and membrane integrity critical for DHA synthesis $[21]$ .

Therefore, the tests in the 5-L bioreactor using the optimized multi-stage process not only validated the enhanced fatty acid production capacity in the acclimated strain but also achieved considerable TFA and DHA yields among existing microbial fermentation studies.

In addition, the expression of genes encoding key enzymes in the antioxidant system, such as superoxide dismutase (SOD), glutathione peroxidase (GPX), and catalase (CAT), was also up-regulated (Additional file 1: Table S5), which could inhibit the conversion of PUFAs to SFAs to a certain extent.

The multi-modular synthesis mechanism of PKS (such as Type I PKS) that directly elongates the carbon chain through iterative condensation reactions can also reduce NADPH consumption (the FAS pathway requires 2 molecules of NADPH per round of extension, while PKS only requires 1 molecule).

In the pentose phosphate pathway, the expression of genes encoding glucose-6-phosphate dehydrogenase (G6PD) and 6-phosphogluconate dehydrogenase (6PGD), which are the key enzymes responsible for the production of NADPH, were up-regulated, indicating that the yield of NADPH could
We selected six databases (GO, KEGG, Pfam, swissprot, eggNOG, and NR) to compare and annotate the assembled unigenes (Additional file 1: Table S4).

event_graph.paper_main_problem: Wild-type Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 has low DHA yield due to poor adaptation to low pH during fermentation.

canonical_events.summary: Wild-type Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 fails to meet commercial DHA yields due to poor adaptation to low pH during fermentation.
Hypothesis that multi-factor acclimation (low pH, low temperature, high DO) will synergistically enhance DHA production through complex metabolic rewiring.
Orthogonal experimental design used to combine two temperatures, two DO levels, and three acid types for 12 ALE conditions.
Staged acidic ALE process: pH decreased from 5.5 to 3.5 over 75 passages (150 days).
Wild-type and 12 ALE endpoint strains cultured under standard conditions to measure DCW, TFA, DHA.
Citric acid acclimated strains showed higher biomass than other acids, indicating organic acid tolerance role.
Low temperature or high DO alone did not further increase biomass beyond citric acid alone.
Low temperature and high DO increased TFA but not biomass, confirming they enhance fatty acid production via cellular physiology.
DHA/TFA fraction decreased in most ALE strains; only citric and acetic acid at normal conditions increased DHA fraction.
High DO may cause oxidative degradation of DHA, reducing DHA/TFA fraction.
Transcriptomic analysis of ALE_AC_11 and wild-type strains at 36 h and 48 h to identify differentially expressed genes.
KEGG pathway enrichment analysis of DEGs at 36 h revealed enrichment in carbohydrate, amino acid, lipid, and energy metabolism.
Upregulation of GK and GPD in ALE_AC_11 despite no glycerol in medium suggests potential internal glycerol synthesis or metabolic readiness.
Downregulation of AGPAT and terpenoid synthesis genes in ALE_AC_11 suggests redirection of acetyl-CoA flux toward fatty acid synthesis.
At 48 h, PFK downregulation and G6PD upregulation in ALE_AC_11 suggest decreased upper glycolysis flux and increased PPP flux, lowering ATP/NADPH ratio.
Specific genetic mutations responsible for transcriptional changes remain undetermined; whole-genome sequencing needed.
Disproportionate DHA increase suggests ALE improved DHA biosynthesis efficiency per unit biomass, not just biomass accumulation.
Bioreactor productivity gains were smaller than flask cultures, possibly due to reduced oxygen transfer and mixing limitations.

edges.reason: Problem of low DHA yield motivates hypothesis for multi-factor acclimation.
Hypothesis leads to design of orthogonal experimental ALE conditions.
Orthogonal design is implemented via staged acidic ALE process.
ALE process yields endpoint strains for characterization.
Characterization reveals citric acid strains have higher biomass.
Further analysis shows low temperature or high DO alone does not increase biomass beyond citric acid.
Despite no biomass increase, low temperature and high DO increase TFA.
TFA increase leads to examination of DHA/TFA fraction, revealing decrease in most strains.
Decreased DHA/TFA fraction is partly attributed to oxidative degradation by high DO.
Selection of optimal strain ALE_AC_11 for transcriptomic analysis.
Transcriptomic data enables KEGG pathway enrichment analysis.
Pathway analysis reveals upregulation of GK and GPD.
Pathway analysis reveals downregulation of AGPAT and terpenoid synthesis.
Temporal transcriptomic comparison reveals metabolic shift at 48 h.
Transcriptional changes suggest genetic mutations, but they remain undetermined.
Bioreactor validation shows disproportionate DHA increase, indicating improved biosynthesis efficiency.
Bioreactor scale-up reveals limitations in productivity gains.

must_cover_points: Wild-type low DHA yield due to poor low-pH adaptation.
Multi-factor ALE hypothesis (low pH, low temperature, high DO).
Orthogonal design and staged acidic ALE process.
Citric acid acclimation yields higher biomass.
Low temperature/high DO increase TFA but not biomass.
DHA/TFA fraction decreases in most ALE strains.
Transcriptomic analysis of ALE_AC_11 at 36 h and 48 h.
Upregulation of glycolysis, TCA, PPP, PKS pathways.
GK and GPD upregulation suggests glycerol utilization potential.
Temporal metabolic shift: PFK down, G6PD up at 48 h.
Bioreactor validation with improved DHA biosynthesis efficiency.
Limitations: genetic mutations unknown, scale-up challenges.
